meta.title: Binary Search
meta.intro: You warmed these up on paper in the Trace the Shelf and Sorted or Not? exercises — same sorted shelf, now on screen. Type and run each one; they get harder as you go, ending with one you build from scratch. Everything here stays at the halving / iteration-count level: find the middle, eliminate half, count the checks. Predict the output first, then verify.

problems.prompt: The shelf holds 15 books sorted by call number: [3, 8, 12, 15, 21, 26, 33, 40, 47, 52, 58, 61, 70, 75, 88]. Binary search always checks the MIDDLE book first. Print the value of that first middle book.
problems.promptHtml: The shelf holds 15 books sorted by call number: [3, 8, 12, 15, 21, 26, 33, 40, 47, 52, 58, 61, 70, 75, 88]. Binary search always checks the MIDDLE book first. Print the value of that first middle book.

starter.python:
shelf = [3, 8, 12, 15, 21, 26, 33, 40, 47, 52, 58, 61, 70, 75, 88]
# binary search checks the MIDDLE first
# find the middle index of the 15-item list, then print the value there

problems.hint: For 15 items the middle is the 8th book (position 8 in the 1-indexed exam notation). In Python and JavaScript, lists are 0-indexed, so len(shelf) // 2 gives index 7 — the same middle book. Print the value stored there, not the index.

problems.prompt: Count the WORST-CASE number of checks for the 15-book shelf. Start with 15 items and repeatedly halve the count (throwing away half each check) until nothing is left, counting each halving. Print the total number of checks.
problems.promptHtml: Count the WORST-CASE number of checks for the 15-book shelf. Start with 15 items and repeatedly halve the count (throwing away half each check) until nothing is left, counting each halving. Print the total number of checks.

starter.python:
size = 15      # items still in play
checks = 0    # checks made so far
# each check lets you throw away half: halve size and add 1 to checks
# keep going until size reaches 0, then print checks

reference.pseudocode:
size ← 15
checks ← 0
REPEAT UNTIL(size < 1)
{
  checks ← checks + 1
  size ← size / 2
}
DISPLAY(checks)
reference.python:
size = 15
checks = 0
while size > 0:
    checks = checks + 1
    size = size // 2
print(checks)

problems.hint: Halve with whole-number division so you count whole books: 15 → 7 → 3 → 1 → 0. That is 4 halvings, so 4 checks. Add 1 to checks each time BEFORE (or after) you halve — just make sure the loop stops when size hits 0.

problems.prompt: Show the elimination. Starting from the 15-book shelf, print the number of books still in play AFTER each check (each check throws away half), one number per line, until none remain.
problems.promptHtml: Show the elimination. Starting from the 15-book shelf, print the number of books still in play AFTER each check (each check throws away half), one number per line, until none remain.

starter.python:
size = 15
# each check throws away half of what's left
# after each check, print how many books remain
# stop once nothing remains

reference.python:
size = 15
while size > 0:
    size = size // 2
    print(size)

problems.hint: Halve first, then print the new size: 15 halves to 7 (print 7), then 3, then 1, then 0 (print 0 and stop). The four numbers you print line up with the 4 checks from the previous problem.

problems.prompt: Build it from scratch. A different library shelf holds 1000 books, sorted. Print the worst-case number of checks binary search needs to find a book — count how many times 1000 halves down to nothing.
problems.promptHtml: Build it from scratch. A different library shelf holds 1000 books, sorted. Print the worst-case number of checks binary search needs to find a book — count how many times 1000 halves down to nothing.

starter.python:
# A sorted shelf of 1000 books.
# Count the worst-case checks by halving 1000 until nothing is left.
# Print just the number of checks.

reference.pseudocode:
size ← 1000
checks ← 0
REPEAT UNTIL(size < 1)
{
  checks ← checks + 1
  size ← size / 2
}
DISPLAY(checks)
reference.python:
size = 1000
checks = 0
while size > 0:
    checks = checks + 1
    size = size // 2
print(checks)

problems.hint: Same halving loop as the 15-book shelf, just starting at 1000: 1000 → 500 → 250 → 125 → 62 → 31 → 15 → 7 → 3 → 1 → 0. Count the steps — 10. Linear search would need up to 1000 checks on the same shelf, which is the whole point.
